draft.subject: the part none of the robotics companies started from
draft.body: Hi Pannag — SAS-Prompt read to me like the honest next step after RT-X: once you've pooled cross-embodiment data, the self-improvement loop on top of it becomes the real bottleneck. I invest at inception and have spent serious time on the post-DeepMind wave (Physical Intelligence, Skild, Generalist, Figure's Helix) — and the part none of them started from is the thing you actually pulled off: getting 21 competing labs to pool their data is rarer than any single model. I'd love to hear how you're weighing the data flywheel against the dynamic-control gap your table-tennis work exposed — 45% vs. humans is the most honest agility number anyone's published. I can write $500K–$1M, but mostly I'd just like to know what you're building. — Vansh

draft.subject: World Action Models, and where the representation lives
draft.body: Hi Scott — I partner with out-of-distribution people solving truly hard problems in AI, and I saw you're building something new. World Action Models stuck with me — the policy emerging from predicting how the world evolves, rather than trained separately like VLAs or GATO. I think that's the right frame. Where my head goes next is the representation: DreamZero and Cosmos predict in video space, but the JEPA bet is to predict in latent space instead — throw away the pixel detail that doesn't matter and model the abstract dynamics. I'm genuinely curious whether you see video as the representation that works, or a stepping stone toward predicting in representation space. (WAM almost feels like your 2016 generative work and the GATO thesis finally fused.) I write $500K–$1M at inception, but mostly I'd love to hear how you're thinking about it. — Vansh
